title: Fix 'Backend connection unavailable' error firing immediately on Publish click

requirements.text: In StepPublish.tsx, replace the current actor readiness guard with a fully reactive implementation: subscribe to the actor reference via a useEffect or useMemo that watches the actor value from useActor; derive an `isActorReady` boolean that is only true when the actor is confirmed non-null and the Internet Identity session is active. Disable the 'Publish Invitation' button and show a 'Connecting to backend…' spinner/label whenever `isActorReady` is false. The publish handler must perform an explicit null-check on the actor at invocation time and abort with a user-facing inline error message if it is still null — it must never call the canister with a null actor.

quotes: it shown immediately when I click 'Publish Invitation' (before any progress start)
Making the readiness check truly reactive — watching the actor reference and blocking the publish handler until it's confirmed non-null and the Internet Identity session is active
Adding a clear 'Connecting to backend...' state on the button while waiting

acceptanceCriteria: The 'Publish Invitation' button is disabled and shows 'Connecting to backend…' text/spinner from the moment the StepPublish component mounts until the actor reference is confirmed non-null.
Clicking the button while the actor is null does nothing — no error toast, no canister call, no hang.
Once the actor becomes non-null, the button becomes enabled automatically without requiring a page refresh.

If the actor is null at the moment the publish handler fires (race condition), a clear inline error message is displayed ('Backend not ready — please wait a moment and try again') and the canister call is not attempted.
The spinner/disabled state does not flash unnecessarily if the actor initializes quickly — it should resolve smoothly.

requirements.text: In CreateInvitePage.tsx, add a top-level actor readiness guard in the handlePublish function: before assembling the InvitePayload or making any canister call, check that the actor instance is strictly non-null. If the actor is null at this point, immediately abort the publish flow, display a clear inline error message to the user ('Backend connection unavailable — please wait a moment and try again'), and return early. This guard is a second line of defense in addition to the button-level guard in StepPublish.tsx.

quotes: Ensuring CreateInvitePage.tsx also guards the publish call with an explicit actor null-check and surfaces a friendly retry message if it fires too early

acceptanceCriteria: handlePublish in CreateInvitePage.tsx returns early with a visible error if the actor is null at call time.
The error message is shown inline (not just in the browser console) so the user knows what happened.
No canister call is attempted when the actor is null.
The normal publish flow is unaffected when the actor is properly initialized.

requirements.text: In frontend/src/hooks/useQueries.ts, audit all mutation hooks (useCreateInvite, useUpdateInvite, and any other hook that calls the actor). Ensure each mutation function: (1) receives the actor as a dependency that is watched reactively, (2) performs an explicit non-null assertion on the actor before any canister call, and (3) throws or returns a descriptive error object (e.g., { error: 'Actor not initialized' }) if the actor is null — rather than throwing a generic JS error or calling a null reference. This ensures the error propagates gracefully to the calling component.

quotes: Making the readiness check truly reactive — watching the actor reference and blocking the publish handler until it's confirmed non-null

acceptanceCriteria: useCreateInvite and useUpdateInvite return a descriptive error (not an unhandled exception) when the actor is null.
The error message clearly states the actor is not yet initialized.
No TypeError or 'Cannot read properties of null' errors appear in the console when publishing.
Mutation hooks work correctly when the actor is properly initialized.

constraints: Do not modify any files under frontend/src/hooks/useInternetIdentity.ts, frontend/src/hooks/useActor.ts, frontend/src/main.tsx, or frontend/src/components/ui.
Do not change the backend Motoko actor or any backend endpoints.
Do not alter the publish payload structure or the chunked upload flow — only the actor readiness gating logic should change.
nonGoals: Changing how the actor is created or authenticated — only how its readiness is communicated to the UI.
Modifying the chunked image upload logic or InvitePayload assembly.
Changing any template, RSVP form, or invitation page components.
